title: Project Overview
description: The README is the authoritative map of the entire system: it explains ECC's identity as a cross-harness agent operating system, enumerates the seven major component kinds (Skills, Agents, Commands, Rules, Hooks, Automation, Rust/Python layers), and describes the three-tier install model (modules → components → profiles). Reading it first establishes the vocabulary used throughout every other file in the tour. The companion SOUL.md distills ECC's philosophical core — the principles and agent-orchestration philosophy that explain why the architecture is shaped the way it is.
languageLesson: ECC is a markdown-dominant codebase: Skills, Agents, Commands, and Rules are all spec-driven Markdown files rather than executable code. The plugin runtime reads these specs and injects them into agent context, so the 'source of truth' for most functionality is a SKILL.md or command .md, not a .js or .py file.

title: Plugin Manifest & npm Package
description: Two config files define ECC as an installable artifact. The plugin.json is the Claude Code plugin manifest: it declares plugin identity, the directories that contain skills and commands, hook registrations, and settings schema — it is what Claude Code reads when a user installs ECC via `/install ecc@ecc`. The package.json alongside it is the npm manifest for `ecc-universal`, listing every file that ships to npm and the scripts that drive the CI/CD pipeline. Together they answer the question: how does ECC get from a git repo into a running agent session?
languageLesson: Claude Code plugins use a plugin.json manifest to declare capabilities without any code: a skills directory path, a commands path, and hook entry points are all that's needed to extend the harness. This manifest-first design means ECC's 271 skills and 92 commands are registered purely through directory conventions.

title: Skills Catalog — the Dominant Surface
description: Skills are the largest component kind in ECC (271 files) and the primary interface users interact with. CLAUDE.md at the repo root is the in-session instruction file that describes which skills exist and how to invoke them; AGENTS.md expands on this for the 67 named subagent definitions; and CONTRIBUTING.md shows exactly how a new skill is added — the template convention, required frontmatter, and wiring into plugin.json. This trio covers the full lifecycle of a skill: what it is, how it runs, and how to extend the catalog.
languageLesson: ECC skills follow a spec-driven pattern: each SKILL.md describes a capability in natural language with a structured preamble. The harness reads this file and injects it into agent context at invocation time — there is no compilation step. This makes skills auditable, diffable, and writable by non-engineers.

title: Commands & Rules
description: Beyond skills, ECC exposes 92 slash commands (COMMANDS-QUICK-REF.md) and 114 per-language coding-standard rules (RULES.md). Commands give users imperative actions (/plan, /review, /ship, etc.) while rules inject language-specific constraints (Python style, Rust idioms, TypeScript patterns) into every agent session. These two layers together shape the agent's behavior at the command surface and at the code-generation level — they are the 'policy layer' of the OS.

title: Install Pipeline — Core Scripts
description: The Node.js automation layer (scripts/) is ECC's operational backbone. The fan-in analysis identifies `utils.js` (24 inbound edges) as the most-depended-upon file in the entire codebase — it provides path resolution, file I/O, git helpers, and process execution used by every other script. `install-manifests.js` (16 inbound edges) is the manifest loader that reads ECC's three-tier install model (modules/components/profiles) from disk. `install-executor.js` is the engine that materializes an install plan across all supported targets (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Antigravity). These three files form the core of the install pipeline.
languageLesson: The install pipeline uses a plan-then-execute pattern: `install-manifests.js` resolves what needs to be installed from declarative YAML manifests, `install-executor.js` turns that plan into file-system operations across multiple harness targets. This separation makes the install logic testable in isolation from file-system side effects.

title: Install Targets & Lifecycle
description: Building on the core engine from Step 5, `install-targets/registry.js` (the highest fan-out file in the codebase at 12 outbound edges) is the central registry of every harness target ECC can install into — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, and more. Each adapter knows how to write config files, symlinks, and manifests for its specific target. `install-lifecycle.js` wraps the whole pipeline in discovery, health-check, repair, and uninstall operations, persisting state via `install-state.js`. This is how ECC becomes idempotent and self-healing.

title: Lifecycle Hooks
description: Hooks are ECC's event-driven behavior layer — they run at session start, before/after bash commands, and before/after tool use. The `bash-hook-dispatcher.js` (highest fan-out hook file, 11 outbound edges) is the central router that composes individual hook handlers into a pipeline for PreBash and PostBash events. `session-start.js` injects rich context (recent session history, learned skills, MCP inventory) into every new Claude session. `pretooluse-visible-output.js` provides the shared utility that lets hooks attach `additionalContext` to tool calls. Together these three files implement ECC's 'always-on intelligence' — the ambient enhancements every session gets for free.
languageLesson: Claude Code hooks receive structured JSON on stdin and must respond with JSON on stdout. ECC's dispatcher pattern composes multiple handlers — each a pure function of the hook payload — into a single hook entry point, keeping individual handlers small and independently testable.

title: Session Adapters & MCP Inventory
description: Two tightly coupled clusters (identified by the topology analysis) handle cross-harness session data. The session adapters cluster (`canonical-session.js`, `registry.js`, and harness-specific adapters) normalizes session history from Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, and tmux into a single canonical schema — enabling ECC to inject cross-harness context regardless of which tool the user is running. The MCP inventory cluster (`canonical-mcp.js`, `collect.js`, harness readers) performs the same normalization for MCP server configurations. Both clusters follow the same registry-and-adapter pattern as the install targets.

title: Rust Control Plane (ecc2/)
description: The `ecc2/` directory is ECC's high-performance native layer, a Rust binary named `ecc-tui`. `main.rs` is the CLI entry point implementing the full control-plane surface via clap subcommands: session management, schedules, remote dispatch, worktree control, memory graph, and observability export. `config/mod.rs` (9 inbound edges — one of the most-imported files in the Rust layer) defines all runtime config structs that drive agent behavior. `tui/dashboard.rs` implements the interactive terminal dashboard. `session/store.rs` provides the SQLite-backed persistence layer. The Cargo.toml manifest defines this as the `ecc-tui` crate with TUI and async-runtime dependencies.
languageLesson: Rust's module system maps directly to the filesystem: `config/mod.rs` is the public interface for the entire `config` module. The `ecc2` crate uses Tokio for async I/O and Ratatui for the terminal UI — two idioms common in Rust CLI tools where performance and low latency matter.

title: Python LLM Abstraction Layer (src/)
description: The `src/llm/` package is ECC's provider-agnostic AI inference layer. `core/types.py` (9 inbound edges — the most-imported Python file) defines the canonical type system: Role, ProviderType, message and tool-call dataclasses. `core/interface.py` defines the abstract `LLMProvider` base class and the full error hierarchy, establishing the contract every provider must implement. `providers/resolver.py` reads environment variables to instantiate the correct provider at runtime (Claude, OpenAI, Ollama, astraflow). The `__main__.py` entry point enables `python -m llm` invocation from CI scripts and hooks.
languageLesson: The abstract base class + resolver pattern is the standard Python approach to provider switching without if-chains: `interface.py` defines the contract, each provider in `providers/` implements it, and `resolver.py` picks the right one at runtime from environment config. Adding a new LLM provider requires only a new class and a line in the resolver.

title: CI/CD & Supply-Chain Security
description: ECC's GitHub Actions pipelines close the loop from code to shipped artifact. `ci.yml` runs the full test suite across a 3×3×4 OS/Node/package-manager matrix — the broadest compatibility gate in the repo. `release.yml` handles tag-triggered publishing to npm including an IOC (indicators of compromise) supply-chain scan before any artifact is published. `generator-generic-ossf-slsa3-publish.yml` generates SLSA level-3 provenance for every release, providing a cryptographically verifiable build chain. `supply-chain-watch.yml` runs every 6 hours to continuously verify npm registry signatures. This pipeline reflects ECC's identity as infrastructure software where supply-chain integrity is non-negotiable.
languageLesson: GitHub Actions reusable workflows (`workflow_call`) let ECC factor the test and release logic into `reusable-test.yml` and `reusable-release.yml`, then call them from both CI and release pipelines without duplication. SLSA provenance is generated as a separate workflow that triggers on `release: published` — decoupling build verification from the release process itself.
